annotations.label: Plane

anchor.snippet: a plane is a flat two- dimensional surface that extends indefinitely

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Coplanar` (a predicate on point sets via 2-dimensional vectorSpan) and general `AffineSubspace`, but no bundled `Plane` type.

annotations.label: Plane segment / planar region

anchor.snippet: A plane segment or planar region

annotations.note: No `planeSegment`/`planarRegion` notion was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bivector

anchor.snippet: A bivector is an oriented plane segment

annotations.note: Searching for `bivector`/`Bivector` returns no files in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Face

anchor.snippet: A face is a plane segment bounding a solid object

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a `face` of a solid (only convex-polytope faces in unrelated combinatorial contexts).

annotations.label: Slab

anchor.snippet: A slab is a region bounded by two parallel planes

annotations.note: No `slab`/`Slab` definition was located in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parallelepiped

anchor.snippet: A parallelepiped is a region bounded by three pairs of parallel planes

annotations.note: `parallelepiped v` is defined as the closed parallelepiped spanned by a finite family of vectors.

annotations.label: Cartesian plane

anchor.snippet: equipped with a chosen Cartesian coordinate system is called a Cartesian plane

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)` plays the role of the Cartesian plane, but there is no dedicated `CartesianPlane` definition.

annotations.label: Polar plane

anchor.snippet: a non-Cartesian Euclidean plane equipped with a polar coordinate system would be called a polar plane

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `polarCoord` as a chart on ℝ², but does not bundle a `PolarPlane` structure.

annotations.label: Plane is a ruled surface

anchor.snippet: A plane is a ruled surface

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of `ruled surface`.

annotations.label: Euclidean plane

anchor.snippet: a Euclidean plane is a Euclidean space of dimension two

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)` realizes the Euclidean plane, but there's no named `EuclideanPlane` abbreviation.

annotations.label: Standard Euclidean plane

anchor.snippet: the ordered pairs of real numbers (the real coordinate plane ), equipped with the dot product , is often called the Euclidean plane or standard Euclidean plane

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)` is `PiLp 2 (Fin 2 → ℝ)` with the dot product, realizing the standard Euclidean plane.

annotations.label: Determination of a plane

anchor.section: Determination by contained points and lines
anchor.snippet: a plane is uniquely determined by any of the following

annotations.note: No theorem characterizing the unique affine 2-plane through three non-collinear points (etc.) was found.

annotations.label: Two planes parallel or intersect in a line

anchor.snippet: Two distinct planes are either parallel or they intersect in a line

annotations.note: `AffineSubspace.Parallel` exists but no theorem stating two distinct 2-dimensional subspaces in 3-space are parallel or meet in a line.

annotations.label: Line-plane configurations

anchor.snippet: A line is either parallel to a plane, intersects it at a single point, or is contained in the plane

annotations.note: No theorem classifying line-plane intersection cases in 3-space was located.

annotations.label: Lines perpendicular to same plane are parallel

anchor.snippet: Two distinct lines perpendicular to the same plane must be parallel to each other

annotations.note: No such perpendicularity-parallel theorem for lines/planes was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Planes perpendicular to same line are parallel

anchor.snippet: Two distinct planes perpendicular to the same line must be parallel to each other

annotations.note: No theorem about two planes perpendicular to a common line being parallel was found.

annotations.label: Point–normal form of a plane

anchor.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchor.snippet: which is the point–normal form of the equation of a plane

annotations.note: `signedDist v p q = ⟪normalize v, q -ᵥ p⟫` captures the point-normal expression, but Mathlib has no dedicated `pointNormalForm` definition.

anchors.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchors.snippet: which is the point–normal form of the equation of a plane

annotations.label: Converse: linear equation defines a plane

anchor.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchor.snippet: Conversely, it is easily shown that if

annotations.note: No converse statement identifying solution sets of linear equations with hyperplanes in this form was found.

anchors.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchors.snippet: Conversely, it is easily shown that if

annotations.label: General form of the plane equation

anchor.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchor.snippet: This familiar equation for a plane is called the general form of the equation of the plane

annotations.note: No `ax+by+cz+d=0` general-form-of-a-plane definition was found in Mathlib.

anchors.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchors.snippet: This familiar equation for a plane is called the general form of the equation of the plane

annotations.label: Regression best-fit plane

anchor.section: Point–normal form and general form of the equation of a plane
anchor.snippet: Thus for example a regression equation of the form

annotations.note: Linear regression / best-fit plane is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parametric description of a plane

anchor.section: Describing a plane with a point and two vectors lying on it
anchor.snippet: a plane may be described parametrically as the set of all points of the form

annotations.note: The parametric form is the image of an affine map / `affineSpan` of three points, but no dedicated parametric-plane definition exists.

anchors.section: Describing a plane with a point and two vectors lying on it
anchors.snippet: a plane may be described parametrically as the set of all points of the form

annotations.label: Three non-collinear points

anchor.section: Describing a plane through three points
anchor.snippet: be non-collinear points

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Collinear` (and its negation expresses non-collinearity), but no dedicated `nonCollinear` predicate.

annotations.label: Plane through three points (determinant)

anchor.snippet: The plane passing through

annotations.note: No determinant-equation characterization of a plane through three points was found.

annotations.label: Plane through three points (Cramer's rule)

anchor.snippet: This system can be solved using Cramer's rule

annotations.note: Cramer's rule exists in Mathlib (`Matrix.cramer`) but its application to the plane-through-three-points system is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: This system can be solved using Cramer's rule

annotations.label: Plane through three points (normal via cross product)

anchor.snippet: A suitable normal vector is given by the cross product

annotations.note: `crossProduct` is defined in ℝ³ and `crossProduct_ne_zero_iff_linearIndependent` is available, but the explicit construction of the plane-through-three-points normal is not stated.

anchors.snippet: A suitable normal vector is given by the cross product

annotations.label: Distance from a point to a plane

anchor.section: Distance from a point to a plane
anchor.snippet: the distance from a point to a plane is the distance between a given point and its orthogonal projection on the plane

annotations.note: Mathlib defines orthogonal projection onto an affine subspace and `signedDist`, from which point-to-plane distance follows, but no dedicated `distToPlane` lemma is given.

annotations.label: Distance formula via shifted plane

anchor.section: Distance from a point to a plane
anchor.snippet: The distance between the origin and the point

annotations.note: No closed-form `|ax+by+cz+d|/√(a²+b²+c²)` distance formula was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line–plane intersection cases

anchor.snippet: the intersection of a line and a plane in three-dimensional space can be the empty set

annotations.note: No theorem enumerating the three line-plane intersection cases was located.

annotations.label: Intersection of two planes

anchor.section: Line of intersection between two planes
anchor.snippet: the intersection of two planes in three-dimensional space is a line or the empty set for parallel planes

annotations.note: No statement classifying the intersection of two affine 2-planes in 3-space was found.

annotations.label: Sphere–plane intersection is a circle

anchor.snippet: When the intersection of a sphere and a plane is not empty or a single point, it is a circle

annotations.note: Although `EuclideanGeometry.Sphere` exists, the theorem that a sphere-plane intersection (when more than a point) is a circle is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unique circle through three points on a sphere

anchor.snippet: on a sphere there is exactly one circle that can be drawn through three given points

annotations.note: No theorem asserting uniqueness of a circle through three given points on a sphere was found.

annotations.label: Points of a circle equidistant from its poles

anchor.snippet: the points on a circle are all a common angular distance from one of its poles

annotations.note: Spherical poles and angular distance to a small circle are not formalized.

annotations.label: Attitude / Miller indices

anchor.snippet: The attitude of a lattice plane is the orientation of the line normal to the plane

annotations.note: Miller indices and crystallographic lattice planes have no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trend and plunge

anchor.snippet: The trend is the compass direction of the line

annotations.note: Geological `trend`/`plunge` notions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strike and dip

anchor.snippet: A strike line is the intersection of a horizontal plane with the observed planar feature

annotations.note: Geological `strike`/`dip` notions are not formalized in Mathlib.
